speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Where is my gracious Lord of Canterbury?

speaker: EXETER
text: Not here in presence.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Send for him, good uncle.

speaker: WESTMORELAND
text: Shall we call in th’ Ambassador, my liege?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Not yet, my cousin. We would be resolved,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Before we hear him, of some things of weight

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That task our thoughts concerning us and France.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: God and his angels guard your sacred throne

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: And make you long become it.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Sure we thank you.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: My learnèd lord, we pray you to proceed

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And justly and religiously unfold

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Why the law Salic that they have in France

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Or should or should not bar us in our claim.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And God forbid, my dear and faithful lord,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That you should fashion, wrest, or bow your reading,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Or nicely charge your understanding soul

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: With opening titles miscreate, whose right

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Suits not in native colors with the truth;

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: For God doth know how many now in health

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Shall drop their blood in approbation

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Of what your reverence shall incite us to.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Therefore take heed how you impawn our person,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: How you awake our sleeping sword of war.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We charge you in the name of God, take heed,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: For never two such kingdoms did contend

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Without much fall of blood, whose guiltless drops

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Are every one a woe, a sore complaint

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: ’Gainst him whose wrongs gives edge unto the swords

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That makes such waste in brief mortality.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Under this conjuration, speak, my lord,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: For we will hear, note, and believe in heart

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That what you speak is in your conscience washed

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: As pure as sin with baptism.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Then hear me, gracious sovereign, and you peers

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: That owe yourselves, your lives, and services

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To this imperial throne. There is no bar

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To make against your Highness’ claim to France

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: But this, which they produce from Pharamond:

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: “In terram Salicam mulieres ne succedant”

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: (No woman shall succeed in Salic land),

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Which Salic land the French unjustly gloze

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To be the realm of France, and Pharamond

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The founder of this law and female bar.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Yet their own authors faithfully affirm

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: That the land Salic is in Germany,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Between the floods of Sala and of Elbe,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Where Charles the Great, having subdued the Saxons,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: There left behind and settled certain French,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Who, holding in disdain the German women

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: For some dishonest manners of their life,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Established then this law: to wit, no female

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Should be inheritrix in Salic land,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Which “Salic,” as I said, ’twixt Elbe and Sala

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Is at this day in Germany called Meissen.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Then doth it well appear the Salic law

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Was not devisèd for the realm of France,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Nor did the French possess the Salic land

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Until four hundred one and twenty years

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: After defunction of King Pharamond,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Idly supposed the founder of this law,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Who died within the year of our redemption

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Four hundred twenty-six; and Charles the Great

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Subdued the Saxons and did seat the French

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Beyond the river Sala in the year

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Eight hundred five. Besides, their writers say,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: King Pepin, which deposèd Childeric,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Did, as heir general, being descended

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Of Blithild, which was daughter to King Clothair,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Make claim and title to the crown of France.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Hugh Capet also, who usurped the crown

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Of Charles the Duke of Lorraine, sole heir male

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Of the true line and stock of Charles the Great,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To find his title with some shows of truth,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Though in pure truth it was corrupt and naught,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Conveyed himself as th’ heir to th’ Lady Lingare,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Daughter to Charlemagne, who was the son

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To Lewis the Emperor, and Lewis the son

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Of Charles the Great. Also King Lewis the Tenth,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Who was sole heir to the usurper Capet,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Could not keep quiet in his conscience,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Wearing the crown of France, till satisfied

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: That fair Queen Isabel, his grandmother,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Was lineal of the Lady Ermengare,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Daughter to Charles the foresaid Duke of Lorraine:

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: By the which marriage the line of Charles the Great

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Was reunited to the crown of France.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: So that, as clear as is the summer’s sun,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: King Pepin’s title and Hugh Capet’s claim,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: King Lewis his satisfaction, all appear

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To hold in right and title of the female.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: So do the kings of France unto this day,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Howbeit they would hold up this Salic law

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To bar your Highness claiming from the female,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: And rather choose to hide them in a net

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Than amply to imbar their crooked titles

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Usurped from you and your progenitors.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: May I with right and conscience make this claim?

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The sin upon my head, dread sovereign,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: For in the Book of Numbers is it writ:

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: “When the man dies, let the inheritance

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Descend unto the daughter.” Gracious lord,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Stand for your own, unwind your bloody flag,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Look back into your mighty ancestors.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Go, my dread lord, to your great-grandsire’s tomb,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: From whom you claim; invoke his warlike spirit

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: And your great-uncle’s, Edward the Black Prince,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Who on the French ground played a tragedy,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Making defeat on the full power of France

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Whiles his most mighty father on a hill

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Stood smiling to behold his lion’s whelp

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Forage in blood of French nobility.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: O noble English, that could entertain

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: With half their forces the full pride of France

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: And let another half stand laughing by,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: All out of work and cold for action!

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: Awake remembrance of these valiant dead

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: And with your puissant arm renew their feats.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: You are their heir, you sit upon their throne,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: The blood and courage that renownèd them

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: Runs in your veins; and my thrice-puissant liege

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: Is in the very May-morn of his youth,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: Ripe for exploits and mighty enterprises.

speaker: EXETER
text: Your brother kings and monarchs of the Earth

speaker: EXETER
text: Do all expect that you should rouse yourself

speaker: EXETER
text: As did the former lions of your blood.

speaker: WESTMORELAND
text: They know your Grace hath cause and means and might;

speaker: WESTMORELAND
text: So hath your Highness. Never king of England

speaker: WESTMORELAND
text: Had nobles richer, and more loyal subjects,

speaker: WESTMORELAND
text: Whose hearts have left their bodies here in England

speaker: WESTMORELAND
text: And lie pavilioned in the fields of France.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: O, let their bodies follow, my dear liege,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: With blood and sword and fire to win your right,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: In aid whereof we of the spiritualty

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Will raise your Highness such a mighty sum

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: As never did the clergy at one time

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Bring in to any of your ancestors.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We must not only arm t’ invade the French,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: But lay down our proportions to defend

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Against the Scot, who will make road upon us

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: With all advantages.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: They of those marches, gracious sovereign,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Shall be a wall sufficient to defend

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Our inland from the pilfering borderers.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We do not mean the coursing snatchers only,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: But fear the main intendment of the Scot,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Who hath been still a giddy neighbor to us.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: For you shall read that my great-grandfather

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Never went with his forces into France

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: But that the Scot on his unfurnished kingdom

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Came pouring like the tide into a breach

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: With ample and brim fullness of his force,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Galling the gleanèd land with hot assays,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Girding with grievous siege castles and towns,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That England, being empty of defense,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Hath shook and trembled at th’ ill neighborhood.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: She hath been then more feared than harmed, my liege,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: For hear her but exampled by herself:

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: When all her chivalry hath been in France

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: And she a mourning widow of her nobles,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: She hath herself not only well defended

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: But taken and impounded as a stray

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The King of Scots, whom she did send to France

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To fill King Edward’s fame with prisoner kings

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: And make her chronicle as rich with praise

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: As is the ooze and bottom of the sea

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: With sunken wrack and sumless treasuries.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: But there’s a saying very old and true:

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: “If that you will France win,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: Then with Scotland first begin.”

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: For once the eagle England being in prey,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: To her unguarded nest the weasel Scot

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: Comes sneaking and so sucks her princely eggs,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: Playing the mouse in absence of the cat,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              ELY
text: To ’tame and havoc more than she can eat.

speaker: EXETER
text: It follows, then, the cat must stay at home.

speaker: EXETER
text: Yet that is but a crushed necessity,

speaker: EXETER
text: Since we have locks to safeguard necessaries

speaker: EXETER
text: And pretty traps to catch the petty thieves.

speaker: EXETER
text: While that the armèd hand doth fight abroad,

speaker: EXETER
text: Th’ advisèd head defends itself at home.

speaker: EXETER
text: For government, though high and low and lower,

speaker: EXETER
text: Put into parts, doth keep in one consent,

speaker: EXETER
text: Congreeing in a full and natural close,

speaker: EXETER
text: Like music.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Therefore doth heaven divide

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The state of man in divers functions,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Setting endeavor in continual motion,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To which is fixèd as an aim or butt

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Obedience; for so work the honeybees,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Creatures that by a rule in nature teach

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The act of order to a peopled kingdom.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: They have a king and officers of sorts,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Where some like magistrates correct at home,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Others like merchants venture trade abroad,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Others like soldiers armèd in their stings

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Make boot upon the summer’s velvet buds,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Which pillage they with merry march bring home

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To the tent royal of their emperor,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Who, busied in his majesty, surveys

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The singing masons building roofs of gold,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The civil citizens kneading up the honey,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The poor mechanic porters crowding in

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Their heavy burdens at his narrow gate,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The sad-eyed justice with his surly hum

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Delivering o’er to executors pale

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The lazy yawning drone. I this infer:

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: That many things, having full reference

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: To one consent, may work contrariously,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: As many arrows loosèd several ways

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Come to one mark, as many ways meet in one town,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: As many fresh streams meet in one salt sea,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: As many lines close in the dial’s center,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: So may a thousand actions, once afoot,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: End in one purpose and be all well borne

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Without defeat. Therefore to France, my liege!

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Divide your happy England into four,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Whereof take you one quarter into France,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: And you withal shall make all Gallia shake.

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: If we, with thrice such powers left at home,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Cannot defend our own doors from the dog,

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: Let us be worried, and our nation lose

speaker:
BISHOP
               
              OF
               
              CANTERBURY
text: The name of hardiness and policy.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Call in the messengers sent from the Dauphin.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Now are we well resolved, and by God’s help

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And yours, the noble sinews of our power,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: France being ours, we’ll bend it to our awe

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Or break it all to pieces. Or there we’ll sit,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Ruling in large and ample empery

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: O’er France and all her almost kingly dukedoms,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Or lay these bones in an unworthy urn,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Tombless, with no remembrance over them.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Either our history shall with full mouth

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Speak freely of our acts, or else our grave,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Like Turkish mute, shall have a tongueless mouth,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Not worshiped with a waxen epitaph.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Now are we well prepared to know the pleasure

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Of our fair cousin Dauphin, for we hear

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Your greeting is from him, not from the King.

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: May ’t please your Majesty to give us leave

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: Freely to render what we have in charge,

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: Or shall we sparingly show you far off

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: The Dauphin’s meaning and our embassy?

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We are no tyrant, but a Christian king,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Unto whose grace our passion is as subject

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: As is our wretches fettered in our prisons.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Therefore with frank and with uncurbèd plainness

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Tell us the Dauphin’s mind.

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: Thus, then, in few:

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: Your Highness, lately sending into France,

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: Did claim some certain dukedoms in the right

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: Of your great predecessor, King Edward the Third;

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: In answer of which claim, the Prince our master

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: Says that you savor too much of your youth

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: And bids you be advised there’s naught in France

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: That can be with a nimble galliard won;

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: You cannot revel into dukedoms there.

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: He therefore sends you, meeter for your spirit,

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: This tun of treasure and, in lieu of this,

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: Desires you let the dukedoms that you claim

speaker: AMBASSADOR
text: Hear no more of you. This the Dauphin speaks.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: What treasure, uncle?

speaker: EXETER
text: Tennis balls, my liege.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We are glad the Dauphin is so pleasant with us.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: His present and your pains we thank you for.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: When we have matched our rackets to these balls,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We will in France, by God’s grace, play a set

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Shall strike his father’s crown into the hazard.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Tell him he hath made a match with such a wrangler

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That all the courts of France will be disturbed

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: With chases. And we understand him well,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: How he comes o’er us with our wilder days,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Not measuring what use we made of them.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We never valued this poor seat of England,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And therefore, living hence, did give ourself

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: To barbarous license, as ’tis ever common

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That men are merriest when they are from home.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: But tell the Dauphin I will keep my state,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Be like a king, and show my sail of greatness

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: When I do rouse me in my throne of France,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: For that I have laid by my majesty

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And plodded like a man for working days;

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: But I will rise there with so full a glory

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That I will dazzle all the eyes of France,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Yea, strike the Dauphin blind to look on us.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And tell the pleasant prince this mock of his

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Hath turned his balls to gun-stones, and his soul

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Shall stand sore chargèd for the wasteful vengeance

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That shall fly with them; for many a thousand widows

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Shall this his mock mock out of their dear husbands,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Mock mothers from their sons, mock castles down;

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: And some are yet ungotten and unborn

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That shall have cause to curse the Dauphin’s scorn.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: But this lies all within the will of God,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: To whom I do appeal, and in whose name

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Tell you the Dauphin I am coming on,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: To venge me as I may and to put forth

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: My rightful hand in a well-hallowed cause.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: So get you hence in peace. And tell the Dauphin

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: His jest will savor but of shallow wit

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: When thousands weep more than did laugh at it.—

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Convey them with safe conduct.—Fare you well.

speaker: EXETER
text: This was a merry message.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We hope to make the sender blush at it.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Therefore, my lords, omit no happy hour

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That may give furth’rance to our expedition;

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: For we have now no thought in us but France,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Save those to God, that run before our business.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Therefore let our proportions for these wars

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Be soon collected, and all things thought upon

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That may with reasonable swiftness add

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: More feathers to our wings. For, God before,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: We’ll chide this Dauphin at his father’s door.

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: Therefore let every man now task his thought,

speaker:
KING
               
              HENRY
text: That this fair action may on foot be brought.
